text: Git branching is a core concept in version control that helps teams work on different features simultaneously without interfering with each other's code. When starting a new feature, developers create a separate "feature branch" from the main codebase. This keeps their changes isolated and safe from accidental breaks. Once the feature is complete, the branch undergoes review, testing, and is then merged back into the main branch. Often, this process uses a "pull request" — a formal request to review and discuss changes before integration. A typical version control workflow involves regularly pulling updates from the main branch into feature branches to avoid large conflicts later, ensuring efficient collaboration.
